Was formally declared *[Conducător](/wiki/Conduc%C4%83tor "Conducător")* (literally, "Leader") of the state on 6 September 1940, by a royal decree which consecrated a ceremonial role for the monarch.[[3]](#cite_note-3) * b. **[^](#infob)** Area and population according to Ioan Suciu, *Istoria contemporana a României (1918–2005)*.[[4]](#cite_note-AreaPop-4) * c. **[^](#infoc)** The indicator for the localities of Romania (1941).[[5]](#cite_note-Indicator41-5) * d. **[^](#infod)** 1938 GDP in lei amounting to 387.204 billion (20,487 lei per capita at an estimated population of 18.9 million[[6]](#cite_note-books.google.ro-6)) at the 1938 average exchange rate of 1 leu for $0.00732.[[7]](#cite_note-H_vNAAAAMAAJ_1944,_p._4-7) | |

([Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"): *Regatul României*) was a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") that existed from 25 March [[O.S.](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates") 13 March] 1881 with the crowning of prince Karl of Hohenzollern-Sigmaringen as [King](/wiki/King_of_Romania "King of Romania") [Carol I](/wiki/Carol_I_of_Romania "Carol I of Romania") (thus beginning the [Romanian royal family](/wiki/Romanian_royal_family "Romanian royal family")), until 1947 with the abdication of King [Michael I](/wiki/Michael_I_of_Romania "Michael I of Romania") and the [Romanian Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Romania "Parliament of Romania") proclamation of the [Romanian People's Republic](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Romania "Socialist Republic of Romania").

The **Kingdom of Romania** ([Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"): *Regatul României*) was a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") that existed from 25 March [[O.S.](/wiki/Old_Style_and_New_Style_dates "Old Style and New Style dates") 13 March] 1881 with the crowning of prince Karl of Hohenzollern-Sigmaringen as [King](/wiki/King_of_Romania "King of Romania") [Carol I](/wiki/Carol_I_of_Romania "Carol I of Romania") (thus beginning the [Romanian royal family](/wiki/Romanian_royal_family "Romanian royal family")), until 1947 with the abdication of King [Michael I](/wiki/Michael_I_of_Romania "Michael I of Romania") and the [Romanian Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Romania "Parliament of Romania") proclamation of the [Romanian People's Republic](/wiki/Socialist_Republic_of_Romania "Socialist Republic of Romania").

From 1859 to 1877, Romania evolved from a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") of two [principalities](/wiki/Principality "Principality"): ([Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia") and [Wallachia](/wiki/Wallachia "Wallachia")) called the [Unification of Moldavia and Wallachia](/wiki/Unification_of_Moldavia_and_Wallachia "Unification of Moldavia and Wallachia") also known as "The Little Union" under a single prince to an autonomous principality with a [Hohenzollern](/wiki/House_of_Hohenzollern "House of Hohenzollern") monarchy. The country gained its independence from the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") during the [1877–1878 Russo-Turkish War](/wiki/Russo-Turkish_War_(1877%E2%80%931878) "Russo-Turkish War (1877–1878)") (known locally as the [Romanian War of Independence](/wiki/Romanian_War_of_Independence "Romanian War of Independence")), after which it was forced to cede the southern part of [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia") in exchange for [Northern Dobruja](/wiki/Northern_Dobruja "Northern Dobruja"). The kingdom's territory during the reign of King Carol I, between 13 ([O.S.](/wiki/Adoption_of_the_Gregorian_calendar#Adoption_in_Eastern_Europe "Adoption of the Gregorian calendar")) / 25 March 1881 and 27 September ([O.S.](/wiki/Adoption_of_the_Gregorian_calendar#Adoption_in_Eastern_Europe "Adoption of the Gregorian calendar")) / 10 October 1914 is sometimes referred to as the [Romanian Old Kingdom](/wiki/Romanian_Old_Kingdom "Romanian Old Kingdom"), to distinguish it from "[Greater Romania](/wiki/Greater_Romania "Greater Romania")", which included the provinces that became part of the state after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") ([Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia"), [Banat](/wiki/Banat "Banat"), [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina"), and [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania")).

With the exception of the southern halves of [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina") and [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"), these territories were ceded to neighboring countries in 1940, under the pressure of [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") or the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"). Following the abolishment of the 1923 constitution by [King Carol II](/wiki/King_Carol_II "King Carol II") in 1938, the Kingdom of Romania became an [absolute monarchy](/wiki/Absolute_monarchy "Absolute monarchy"), only to become a [military dictatorship](/wiki/Military_dictatorship "Military dictatorship") under [Ion Antonescu](/wiki/Ion_Antonescu "Ion Antonescu") in 1940 after the forced abdication of King Carol II, with his successor, [King Michael I](/wiki/Michael_I_of_Romania "Michael I of Romania") being a figurehead with no effective political power. The country's name was changed to [Legionary Romania](/wiki/National_Legionary_State "National Legionary State").

The disastrous [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") campaign on the side of the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") led to [King Michael's Coup](/wiki/1944_Romanian_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat "1944 Romanian coup d'état") against Ion Antonescu in 1944, as a result of which the Kingdom of Romania became a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") again and switched sides to the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II"), recovering [Northern Transylvania](/wiki/Northern_Transylvania "Northern Transylvania"). The influence of the neighbouring Soviet Union and the policies followed by [Communist](/wiki/Romanian_Communist_Party "Romanian Communist Party")-dominated coalition governments ultimately led to the abolition of the monarchy, with Romania becoming a Soviet [satellite state](/wiki/Satellite_state "Satellite state") as the [People's Republic of Romania](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_Romania "People's Republic of Romania") on the last day of 1947.

See also: [Unification of Moldavia and Wallachia](/wiki/Unification_of_Moldavia_and_Wallachia "Unification of Moldavia and Wallachia") and [Romanian War of Independence](/wiki/Romanian_War_of_Independence "Romanian War of Independence")

The 1859 ascendancy of [Alexandru Ioan Cuza](/wiki/Alexandru_Ioan_Cuza "Alexandru Ioan Cuza") as prince of both [Moldavia](/wiki/Moldavia "Moldavia") and [Wallachia](/wiki/Wallachia "Wallachia") under the nominal[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9) [suzerainty](/wiki/Suzerainty "Suzerainty") of the Ottoman Empire united an identifiably Romanian nation under a single ruler. On 24 January ([O.S.](/wiki/Adoption_of_the_Gregorian_calendar#Adoption_in_Eastern_Europe "Adoption of the Gregorian calendar")) / 5 February 1862, the two principalities were formally united to form the [Principality of Romania](/wiki/Principality_of_Romania "Principality of Romania"), with [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest") as its capital.

On 11 ([O.S.](/wiki/Adoption_of_the_Gregorian_calendar#Adoption_in_Eastern_Europe "Adoption of the Gregorian calendar")) / 23 February 1866 a so-called "[monstrous coalition](/wiki/Monstrous_coalition "Monstrous coalition")", composed of Conservatives and radical Liberals, forced Cuza to abdicate. The [German prince](/wiki/German_prince "German prince") (and [Prussian](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") officer) Karl Eitel Friedrich Zephyrinus Ludwig von Hohenzollern-Sigmaringen was appointed as Prince of Romania, in a move to assure Prussian backing to unity and future independence. He immediately adopted the Romanian spelling of his name, *[Carol](/wiki/Carol_I_of_Romania "Carol I of Romania")*, and his cognatic descendants would rule Romania until the overthrow of the monarchy in 1947.

For more than a decade after the formal union of the two principalities, Romania was still nominally a vassal of the Ottoman Empire. However, this was increasingly a legal fiction. Romania had its own flag and anthem, and from 1867 had its own currency as well. Following the [Russo-Turkish War of 1877–1878](/wiki/Russo-Turkish_War_(1877%E2%80%931878) "Russo-Turkish War (1877–1878)"), Romania was recognized as an independent state by the [Treaty of Berlin, 1878](/wiki/Treaty_of_Berlin,_1878 "Treaty of Berlin, 1878") and acquired [Dobruja](/wiki/Dobruja "Dobruja"), although it was forced to surrender southern Bessarabia ([Budjak](/wiki/Budjak "Budjak")) to Russia. On 15 March 1881, as an assertion of full sovereignty, the [Romanian parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Romania "Parliament of Romania") raised the country to the status of a kingdom, and Carol was crowned king on 10 May.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Actul_proclamarii_Regatului_Romania.jpg/250px-Actul_proclamarii_Regatului_Romania.jpg)](/wiki/File:Actul_proclamarii_Regatului_Romania.jpg)

Proclamation Act of the Kingdom of Romania

The new state, squeezed between the [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), [Austro-Hungarian](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Empire "Austro-Hungarian Empire"), and [Russian](/wiki/Imperial_Russia "Imperial Russia") Empires, with Slavic populations on its southwestern, southern, and northeastern borders, the Black Sea due east, and [Hungarian](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary") neighbours on its western and northwestern borders, looked to the West, particularly France, for its cultural, educational, and administrative models.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Abstaining from the [Initial Balkan War](/wiki/First_Balkan_War "First Balkan War") against the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), the Kingdom of Romania entered the [Second Balkan War](/wiki/Second_Balkan_War "Second Balkan War") in June 1913 against the [Tsardom of Bulgaria](/wiki/Third_Bulgarian_Empire "Third Bulgarian Empire"). 330,000 Romanian troops moved across the Danube and into Bulgaria. One army occupied [Southern Dobruja](/wiki/Southern_Dobruja "Southern Dobruja") and another moved into northern Bulgaria to threaten Sofia, helping to bring an end to the war. Romania thus acquired the ethnically mixed territory of Southern Dobruja, which it had desired for years.

In 1916 Romania entered [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") on the [Entente](/wiki/Triple_Entente "Triple Entente") side. Romania engaged in a conflict against [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria") but as a result Bulgarian forces, after a series of successful battles, regained [Dobruja](/wiki/Dobruja "Dobruja"), which had been previously ceded from Bulgaria by the treaty of Bucharest and the [Berlin congress](/wiki/Congress_of_Berlin "Congress of Berlin"). Although the Romanian forces did not fare well militarily, by the end of the war the Austrian and Russian empires were gone; various assemblies proclaimed as representative bodies in [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"), [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia") and [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina") decided on union with Romania. In 1919 by the [Treaty of Saint-Germain](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint-Germain "Treaty of Saint-Germain") and in 1920 by the [Treaty of Trianon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Trianon "Treaty of Trianon") most of the territories claimed were assigned to Romania.

### Old Kingdom (1881–1918)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Romania&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Old Kingdom (1881–1918)")]

Main articles: [Romanian Old Kingdom](/wiki/Romanian_Old_Kingdom "Romanian Old Kingdom") and [Romania during World War I](/wiki/Romania_during_World_War_I "Romania during World War I")

The **Romanian Old Kingdom** ([Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"): *Vechiul Regat* or just *Regat*; German: *Regat* or *Altreich*) is a colloquial term referring to the territory covered by the first independent Romanian [nation state](/wiki/Nation_state "Nation state"), which was composed of the [Danubian Principalities](/wiki/Danubian_Principalities "Danubian Principalities") – Wallachia and Moldavia. It was achieved when, under the auspices of the [Treaty of Paris (1856)](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1856) "Treaty of Paris (1856)"), the [ad hoc Divans](/wiki/Ad_hoc_Divans "Ad hoc Divans") of both countries – which were under [Imperial Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") [suzerainty](/wiki/Suzerainty "Suzerainty") at the time – voted for [Alexander Ioan Cuza](/wiki/Alexander_Ioan_Cuza "Alexander Ioan Cuza") as their prince, thus achieving a *de facto* unification. The region itself is defined by the result of that political act, followed by the inclusion of [Northern Dobruja](/wiki/Northern_Dobruja "Northern Dobruja") in 1878, the proclamation of the Kingdom of Romania in 1881, and the annexation of [Southern Dobruja](/wiki/Southern_Dobruja "Southern Dobruja") in 1913.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Romania1901.JPG/250px-Romania1901.JPG)](/wiki/File:Romania1901.JPG)

1901 German map of Romania

The term came into use after World War I, when the Old Kingdom was opposed to [Greater Romania](/wiki/Greater_Romania "Greater Romania"), which included Transylvania, [Banat](/wiki/Banat "Banat"), Bessarabia, and Bukovina. Nowadays, the term is mainly of historical relevance, and is otherwise used as a common term for all regions in Romania included in both the Old Kingdom and present-day borders (namely: Wallachia, Moldavia, and Northern Dobruja).

### World War I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Romania&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: World War I")]

Main article: [Romania in World War I](/wiki/Romania_in_World_War_I "Romania in World War I")

Romania delayed in entering World War I, but ultimately declared war on the Central Powers in 1916. The [Romanian military campaign](/wiki/Romania_in_World_War_I "Romania in World War I") ended in stalemate when the Central Powers quickly crushed the country's [offensive into Transylvania](/wiki/Battle_of_Transylvania "Battle of Transylvania") and occupied Wallachia and Dobruja, including Bucharest and the strategically important oil fields, by the end of 1916. In 1917, despite fierce Romanian resistance, especially at the [Battle of Mărășești](/wiki/Battle_of_M%C4%83r%C4%83%C8%99e%C8%99ti "Battle of Mărășești"), due to Russia's withdrawal from the war following the [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution"), Romania, being almost completely surrounded by the Central Powers, was forced to also drop from the war, signing the [Armistice of Focșani](/wiki/Armistice_of_Foc%C8%99ani "Armistice of Focșani") and next year, in May 1918, the [Treaty of Bucharest](/wiki/Treaty_of_Bucharest_(1918) "Treaty of Bucharest (1918)"). But after the successful offensive on the [Thessaloniki](/wiki/Thessaloniki "Thessaloniki") front which put Bulgaria out of the war, Romania's government quickly reasserted control and put an army back into the field on 10 November 1918, a day before the war ended in Western Europe. Following the proclamation of the [union of Transylvania with the Kingdom of Romania](/wiki/Union_of_Transylvania_with_Romania "Union of Transylvania with Romania") on 1 December 1918 by the representatives of Transylvanian Romanians gathered at [Alba Iulia](/wiki/Alba_Iulia "Alba Iulia"), Transylvania was soon united with the Kingdom, as was [Bessarabia](/wiki/Bessarabia "Bessarabia") earlier in 1918, since the power vacuum in Russia caused by the civil war there allowed the *[Sfatul Țării](/wiki/Sfatul_%C8%9A%C4%83rii "Sfatul Țării")*, or National Council, to proclaim the [union of Bessarabia with Romania](/wiki/Union_of_Bessarabia_with_Romania "Union of Bessarabia with Romania"). [War with the Hungarian Soviet Republic](/wiki/Hungarian%E2%80%93Romanian_War_of_1919 "Hungarian–Romanian War of 1919") in 1919 resulted in the occupation of Budapest by Romanian troops and the end of [Béla Kun](/wiki/B%C3%A9la_Kun "Béla Kun")'s Bolshevik regime.

### Greater Romania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Romania&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Greater Romania")]

#### Union with Bessarabia, Bukovina and Transylvania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Romania&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Union with Bessarabia, Bukovina and Transylvania")]

Main articles: [Union of Transylvania with Romania](/wiki/Union_of_Transylvania_with_Romania "Union of Transylvania with Romania"), [Union of Bessarabia with Romania](/wiki/Union_of_Bessarabia_with_Romania "Union of Bessarabia with Romania"), and [Union of Bukovina with Romania](/wiki/Union_of_Bukovina_with_Romania "Union of Bukovina with Romania")

See also: [Greater Romania](/wiki/Greater_Romania "Greater Romania")

At the [Paris Peace Conference](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Conference_(1919%E2%80%931920) "Paris Peace Conference (1919–1920)"), Romania received the territories of [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"), part of Banat and other territories from [Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary"), as well as Bessarabia (Eastern Moldavia between [Prut](/wiki/Prut "Prut") and [Dniester](/wiki/Dniester "Dniester") rivers) and Bukovina. In the [Treaty of Trianon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Trianon "Treaty of Trianon"), Hungary renounced in favor of Romania all the claims of the [Austro-Hungarian Monarchy](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Monarchy "Austro-Hungarian Monarchy") over Transylvania.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The union of Romania with [Bukovina](/wiki/Bukovina "Bukovina") was ratified in 1919 in the [Treaty of Saint Germain](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint_Germain "Treaty of Saint Germain"),[[13]](#cite_note-13) and in 1920 some of the Western powers recognized Romanian rule over Bessarabia by the [Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1920) "Treaty of Paris (1920)").[[14]](#cite_note-14) Thus, Romania in 1920 was more than twice the size it had been in 1914. The last territorial change during this period came in 1923, when a few border settlements were exchanged between Romania and [Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia"). The most notable Romanian acquisition was the town of [Jimbolia](/wiki/Jimbolia "Jimbolia"), while the most notable Yugoslav acquisition was the town of [Jaša Tomić](/wiki/Ja%C5%A1a_Tomi%C4%87,_Se%C4%8Danj "Jaša Tomić, Sečanj").[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Romania made no further territorial claims; nonetheless the kingdom's expansion aroused enmity from several of its neighbors, including Bulgaria, the Soviet Union, and especially Hungary.[[a]](#cite_note-19) [Greater Romania](/wiki/Greater_Romania "Greater Romania") now had a significant minority population, especially of [Hungarians](/wiki/Hungarians "Hungarians"), and faced the difficulty of assimilation. Transylvania had significant Hungarian and German population who were accustomed to being the power structure; with a historically contemptuous[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] attitude towards Romanians, they now feared reprisals. Both groups were effectively excluded from politics as the postwar regime passed an edict stating that all personnel employed by the state had to speak Romanian. The new state was also a highly centralized one, so it was unlikely that the Hungarian or German minorities would exercise political influence without personal connections in the government in Bucharest. Despite these policies, the Romanian government permitted both Germans and Hungarians the freedom to have separate schools, publications and judicial hearings in their respective languages.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] These rights were not extended to other minorities, Jews in particular.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/RomaniansInHungary1890.png/250px-RomaniansInHungary1890.png)](/wiki/File:RomaniansInHungary1890.png)

Ethnic map of Romanians within the Kingdom of Hungary in 1890

#### The *interbellum* years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Romania&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: The interbellum years")]

The resulting [Great Union](/wiki/Great_Union "Great Union") did not survive World War II. Until 1938, Romania's governments maintained the form, if not always the substance, of a liberal constitutional monarchy. The [National Liberal Party](/wiki/National_Liberal_Party_(Romania,_1875) "National Liberal Party (Romania, 1875)"), dominant in the years immediately after World War I, became increasingly [clientelist](/wiki/Clientelism "Clientelism") and [nationalist](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism"), and in 1927 was supplanted in power by the [National Peasants' Party](/wiki/National_Peasants%27_Party "National Peasants' Party"). Between 1930 and 1940 there were over 25 separate governments; on several occasions in the last few years before World War II, the rivalry between the fascist [Iron Guard](/wiki/Iron_Guard "Iron Guard") and other political groupings approached the level of a civil war.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Upon the death of King [Ferdinand](/wiki/Ferdinand_of_Romania "Ferdinand of Romania") in 1927, his son, [Prince Carol](/wiki/Carol_II_of_Romania "Carol II of Romania"), was prevented from succeeding him because of previous marital scandals that had resulted in his renunciation of rights to the throne. After living three years in exile, with his brother Nicolae serving as regent and his young son [Michael](/wiki/Michael_I_of_Romania "Michael I of Romania") as king, Carol changed his mind and with the support of the ruling National Peasants' Party he returned and proclaimed himself king.

[Iuliu Maniu](/wiki/Iuliu_Maniu "Iuliu Maniu"), leader of the National Peasants' Party, engineered Carol's return on the basis of a promise that he would forsake his mistress [Magda Lupescu](/wiki/Magda_Lupescu "Magda Lupescu"), and Lupescu herself had agreed to the arrangement. However, it became clear upon Carol's first re-encounter with his former wife, [Elena](/wiki/Helen_of_Greece_and_Denmark "Helen of Greece and Denmark"), that he had no interest in a reconciliation with her, and Carol soon arranged for Magda Lupescu's return to his side. Her unpopularity was a millstone around Carol's neck for the rest of his reign, particularly because she was widely viewed as his closest advisor and confidante. Maniu and his National Peasant Party shared the same general political aims as the Iron Guard: both fought against the corruption and dictatorial policies of King Carol II and the National Liberal Party.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

The [worldwide Great Depression that started in 1929](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") and [was also present in Romania](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_Romania "Great Depression in Romania") destabilised the country. The early 1930s were marked by social unrest, high unemployment, and strikes. In several instances, the Romanian government violently repressed strikes and riots, notably the 1929 miners' strike in [Valea Jiului](/wiki/Valea_Jiului "Valea Jiului") and the strike in the [Grivița](/wiki/Grivi%C8%9Ba "Grivița") railroad workshops. In the mid-1930s, the Romanian economy recovered and the industry grew significantly, although about 80% of Romanians were still employed in agriculture. French economic and political influence was predominant in the early 1920s but then Germany became more dominant, especially in the 1930s.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Paris-expo-1937-pavillon_de_la_Roumanie-10.jpg/250px-Paris-expo-1937-pavillon_de_la_Roumanie-10.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paris-expo-1937-pavillon_de_la_Roumanie-10.jpg)

Romanian pavilion at EXPO Paris 1937

As the 1930s progressed, Romania's already shaky democracy slowly deteriorated toward [fascist](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism") dictatorship. The constitution of 1923 gave the king free rein to dissolve parliament and call elections at will; as a result, Romania experienced over 25 governments in a single decade.

Increasingly, these governments were dominated by a number of [anti-Semitic](/wiki/Anti-Semitism "Anti-Semitism"), ultra-nationalist, and mostly at least quasi-fascist parties. The [National Liberal Party](/wiki/National_Liberal_Party_(Romania,_1875) "National Liberal Party (Romania, 1875)") steadily became more nationalistic than liberal, but nonetheless lost its dominance over Romanian politics. It was eclipsed by parties like the (relatively moderate) National Peasants' Party and its more radical [Romanian Front](/wiki/Romanian_Front "Romanian Front") offshoot, the [National-Christian Defense League](/wiki/National-Christian_Defense_League "National-Christian Defense League") (LANC) and the [Iron Guard](/wiki/Iron_Guard "Iron Guard"). In 1935, LANC merged with the [National Agrarian Party](/wiki/National_Agrarian_Party_(Romania) "National Agrarian Party (Romania)") to form the [National Christian Party](/wiki/National_Christian_Party "National Christian Party") (NCP). The quasi-mystical fascist Iron Guard was an earlier LANC offshoot that, even more than these other parties, exploited nationalist feelings, fear of communism, and resentment of alleged foreign and Jewish domination of the economy.

Already the Iron Guard had embraced the politics of assassination, and various governments had reacted more or less in kind. On 10 December 1933, Liberal prime minister [Ion Duca](/wiki/Ion_Duca "Ion Duca") "dissolved" the Iron Guard, arresting thousands; consequently, 19 days later he was assassinated by Iron Guard legionnaires.

Throughout the 1930s, these nationalist parties had a mutually distrustful relationship with King Carol II. Nonetheless, in December 1937, the king appointed National Christian Party leader, the poet [Octavian Goga](/wiki/Octavian_Goga "Octavian Goga"), as prime minister of Romania's first [Fascist government](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania_under_Fascism "Kingdom of Romania under Fascism"). Around this time, Carol met with [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler"), who expressed his wish to see a Romanian government headed by the pro-Nazi Iron Guard. Instead, on 10 February 1938 King Carol II used the occasion of a public insult by Goga toward Lupescu as a reason to dismiss the government and institute a short-lived royal dictatorship, sanctioned 17 days later by a new constitution under which the king named personally not only the prime minister but all the ministers.

In April 1938, King Carol had Iron Guard leader [Corneliu Zelea Codreanu](/wiki/Corneliu_Zelea_Codreanu "Corneliu Zelea Codreanu") (aka "The Captain") arrested and imprisoned. On the night of 29–30 November 1938, Codreanu and several other legionnaires were killed while purportedly attempting to escape from prison. It is generally agreed that there was no such escape attempt, but that they were murdered in retaliation for a series of assassinations by Iron Guard commandos.

The royal dictatorship was brief. On 7 March 1939, a new government was formed with [Armand Călinescu](/wiki/Armand_C%C4%83linescu "Armand Călinescu") as prime minister; on 21 September 1939, three weeks after the start of World War II, Călinescu, in turn, was also assassinated by legionnaires avenging Codreanu's murder.

In 1939, Germany and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") signed the [Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact](/wiki/Molotov%E2%80%93Ribbentrop_Pact "Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact"), which stipulated, among other things, the Soviet "interest" in Bessarabia. After the 1940 territorial losses and growing increasingly unpopular, Carol was compelled to abdicate and name general [Ion Antonescu](/wiki/Ion_Antonescu "Ion Antonescu") as the new Prime-Minister with full powers in ruling the state by royal decree.[[21]](#cite_note-22)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Romania&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Economy")]

### Pre-Kingdom Era to World War I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Romania&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Pre-Kingdom Era to World War I")]

At the time of the proclamation of the Kingdom, there were already several industrial facilities in the country: The Assan and Olamazu [steam mills](/wiki/Steam_mill "Steam mill"), built in 1853 and 1862 respectively, a brick factory built in 1865, and two sugar factories built in 1873, among others. In 1857, the first oil refinery in the world was built at [Ploiești](/wiki/Ploie%C8%99ti "Ploiești").[[22]](#cite_note-23) In 1880, after several railways were built, the [CFR](/wiki/C%C4%83ile_Ferate_Rom%C3%A2ne "Căile Ferate Române") was founded. After proclamation of the Kingdom, the pre-established industrial facilities began to be highly developed: 6 more, larger, sugar factories were built and the railway network was expanded more. Another, more modern brick factory was built in 1891.[[23]](#cite_note-rumaniamilitary.ro-24)

### Agriculture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Romania&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Agriculture")]

Despite all of these industrial achievements, the overwhelming majority of Romania's economy remained agricultural.[[23]](#cite_note-rumaniamilitary.ro-24) In 1919, a staggering 72% of Romanians were engaged in agriculture. The Romanian peasantry was among the poorest in the region, a situation aggravated by one of Europe's highest birth rates. Farming was primitive and machinery and chemical fertilizers almost unheard of. The Regat (prewar Romania) was traditionally a land of large estates worked by peasants who either had little or no land of their own. The situation in Transylvania and Bessarabia was marginally better. After peasant calls for land reform snowballed into an avalanche, King Ferdinand had to oblige, especially once the Russian Revolution had encouraged peasants to take the matter in their own hands. [The land reform](/wiki/Land_reform_in_Romania#1921_reform "Land reform in Romania") passed in 1921 accomplished little however. Large landowners still controlled up to 30% of Romania's land, including the forests peasants depended on for fuel. The redistributed plots were invariably too small to feed their owners and most peasants could not overcome their tradition of growing grain over cash crops. Nothing was done to remedy basic problems such as rural overpopulation and technological backwardness. Draft animals were rare, to say nothing of machinery, actual productivity was worse than before. Romanian agriculture struggled in the international market,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and with the onset of [the Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression_in_Romania "Great Depression in Romania"), collapsed completely.

### Expansion and growth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Romania&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Expansion and growth")]

Romania's 1913 GDP at the 1990 exchange rate amounted to $11.7 billion.[[24]](#cite_note-25) However, the 1990 dollar was 9.27 times weaker than the 1938 dollar.[[25]](#cite_note-26) Thus, Romania's 1913 GDP at the 1938 exchange rate amounted to $1.262 billion.

The 1938 Romanian GDP amounted to 387.204 billion lei, with a GDP per capita of 20,487 lei at an estimated population of 18.9 million.[[6]](#cite_note-books.google.ro-6) The 1938 average exchange rate was of 1 [leu](/wiki/Romanian_leu "Romanian leu") for US$0.00732.[[7]](#cite_note-H_vNAAAAMAAJ_1944,_p._4-7) Romania's 1938 GDP thus amounted to $2.834 billion.

Romania's public debt as of 1 April 1938 amounted to 112,267,290,144 lei, of which 78,398,078,964 lei consisted of external debt.[[26]](#cite_note-27) Total public debt thus amounted to 29% of Romania's 1938 GDP, while public external debt amounted to just over 20%.

### Industrial development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Romania&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Industrial development")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Romania_CFR_151.002_2-10-2.JPG/250px-Romania_CFR_151.002_2-10-2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Romania_CFR_151.002_2-10-2.JPG)

The Malaxa Prime, a Romanian-made steel-wrought locomotive

Despite the destruction provoked by the First World War, Romanian industry managed significant growth, as a result of new establishments and development of the older ones. The [MALAXA](/wiki/FAUR "FAUR") industrial engineering and manufacturing company was established in 1921 by Romanian industrialist [Nicolae Malaxa](/wiki/Nicolae_Malaxa "Nicolae Malaxa") and dealt especially with rolling stock maintenance and manufacturing.[[27]](#cite_note-28) It developed rapidly, and by 1930 Romania had managed to cease importing locomotives altogether, all required rolling stock being supplied by the local industry.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Industrial facilities acquired along with the new provinces, such as the [Reșița works](/wiki/Re%C8%99i%C8%9Ba_works "Reșița works"), also contributed to the rapid development of Romanian heavy industry. Other important establishments were the [Copșa Mică works](/wiki/Cop%C8%99a_Mic%C4%83_works "Copșa Mică works"), producing non-ferrous metals and the [Romanian Optical Enterprise](/wiki/I.O.R. "I.O.R."). Construction also developed, as great monuments like the [Caraiman Cross](/wiki/Heroes%27_Cross_on_Caraiman_Peak "Heroes' Cross on Caraiman Peak") (1928), [Arcul de Triumf](/wiki/Arcul_de_Triumf "Arcul de Triumf") (1936), and the [Mausoleum of Mărășești](/wiki/Mausoleum_of_M%C4%83r%C4%83%C8%99e%C8%99ti "Mausoleum of Mărășești") (1938) were erected. The oil industry was also greatly expanded, making Romania one of the top oil exporters by the late 1930s, which also attracted German and Italian [interest](/wiki/German%E2%80%93Romanian_Treaty_for_the_Development_of_Economic_Relations_between_the_Two_Countries_(1939) "German–Romanian Treaty for the Development of Economic Relations between the Two Countries (1939)").

In 1938, Romania produced 6.6 million tons of crude oil, 284,000 tons of crude steel, 133,000 tons of [pig iron](/wiki/Pig_iron "Pig iron"), 510,000 tons of cement, and 289,000 tons of rolled steel.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

### Armament industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Romania&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Armament industry")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Mortier_Negrei_250_mm_Model_1916.JPG/250px-Mortier_Negrei_250_mm_Model_1916.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mortier_Negrei_250_mm_Model_1916.JPG)

250 mm Negrei mortar

Romanian military industry during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") was mainly focused on converting various fortification guns into field and anti-aircraft artillery. Up to 334 German 53 mm [Fahrpanzer](/wiki/Fahrpanzer "Fahrpanzer") guns, 93 French 57 mm Hotchkiss guns, 66 Krupp 150 mm guns and dozens more 210 mm guns were mounted on Romanian-built [carriages](/wiki/Gun_carriage "Gun carriage") and transformed into mobile field artillery, with 45 Krupp 75 mm guns and 132 Hotchkiss 57 mm guns being transformed into anti-aircraft artillery. The Romanians also [upgraded](/wiki/10.5_cm_Feldhaubitze_M.12 "10.5 cm Feldhaubitze M.12") 120 German Krupp 105 mm howitzers, the result being the most effective field howitzer in Europe at that time. Romania even managed to design and build from scratch its own model of mortar, the 250 mm Negrei Model 1916.[[29]](#cite_note-30) Other Romanian technological assets include the building of [Vlaicu III](/wiki/A_Vlaicu_III "A Vlaicu III"), the world's first aircraft made of metal.[[30]](#cite_note-31) The Romanian Navy possessed the largest warships on the Danube. They were a class of 4 river monitors, built locally at the [Galați shipyard](/wiki/Gala%C8%9Bi_shipyard "Galați shipyard") using parts manufactured in Austria-Hungary, and the first one launched was *Lascăr Catargiu*, in 1907.[[31]](#cite_note-32)[[32]](#cite_note-33) The Romanian monitors displaced almost 700 tons, were armed with three 120 mm naval guns in 3 turrets, two 120 mm naval howitzers, four 47 mm anti-aircraft guns and two 6.5 machine guns.[[33]](#cite_note-34) The monitors took part in the [Battle of Turtucaia](/wiki/Battle_of_Turtucaia "Battle of Turtucaia") and the [First Battle of Cobadin](/wiki/First_Battle_of_Cobadin "First Battle of Cobadin"). The Romanian-designed Schneider 150 mm Model 1912 howitzer was considered one of the most modern field guns on the Western Front.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/IAR80.jpg/250px-IAR80.jpg)](/wiki/File:IAR80.jpg)

A formation of IAR-80 fighter aircraft

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Amiral_Murgescu_%28side%29.jpg/250px-Amiral_Murgescu_%28side%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Amiral_Murgescu_(side).jpg)

Minelayer [NMS *Amiral Murgescu*](/wiki/NMS_Amiral_Murgescu "NMS Amiral Murgescu")

The Romanian armament industry was expanded greatly during the Interwar period and World War II. New factories were constructed, such as the [Industria Aeronautică Română](/wiki/Industria_Aeronautic%C4%83_Rom%C3%A2n%C4%83 "Industria Aeronautică Română") and [Societatea Pentru Exploatări Tehnice](/wiki/Societatea_Pentru_Exploat%C4%83ri_Tehnice "Societatea Pentru Exploatări Tehnice") aircraft factories, which produced hundreds of indigenous aircraft, such as [IAR 37](/wiki/IAR_37 "IAR 37"), [IAR 80](/wiki/IAR_80 "IAR 80"), and [SET 7](/wiki/SET_7 "SET 7"). Before the war, Romania acquired from France the licence to produce hundreds of [Brandt Mle 27/31](/wiki/Brandt_Mle_27/31 "Brandt Mle 27/31") and [Brandt Mle 1935](/wiki/Brandt_Mle_1935 "Brandt Mle 1935") mortars, with hundreds more produced during the war,[[35]](#cite_note-36) and also the licence to produce 140 French 47 mm Schneider anti-tank guns at the Concordia factory, with 118 produced between 26 May 1939 and 1 August 1940 and hundreds more produced during the war;[[36]](#cite_note-37)[[37]](#cite_note-auto-38) these guns were to be towed by [Malaxa Tip UE](/wiki/Renault_UE_Chenillette "Renault UE Chenillette") armored carriers, built since late 1939 at the Malaxa factory under French licence, eventually 126 being built until March 1941. Czechoslovak licence was acquired in 1938 to produce the [ZB vz. 30](/wiki/ZB_vz._30 "ZB vz. 30") machine gun, with 5,000 being built at the Cugir gun factory until the start of [Operation Barbarossa](/wiki/Operation_Barbarossa "Operation Barbarossa") in June 1941.[[38]](#cite_note-auto1-39) Romania also acquired the licence to produce the [R-1](/wiki/R-1_tank "R-1 tank") tankette, but ultimately only one prototype was built locally.[[39]](#cite_note-40) German licence was acquired in 1938 to produce 360 [37 mm Rheinmetall anti-aircraft guns](/wiki/3.7_cm_Flak_18/36/37/43 "3.7 cm Flak 18/36/37/43"), but only 102 were produced until May 1941.[[38]](#cite_note-auto1-39) British licence was acquired to produce 100 [Vickers Model 1931](/wiki/Vickers_Model_1931 "Vickers Model 1931") 75 mm anti-aircraft guns at the [Reșița works](/wiki/Re%C8%99i%C8%9Ba_works "Reșița works"), with the first battery of 6 guns entering service on 1 August 1939, and 100 more guns were built during the war for a total production of 200.[[38]](#cite_note-auto1-39) On 14 June, Romania launched the first locally-built warship, the minelayer [NMS *Amiral Murgescu*](/wiki/NMS_Amiral_Murgescu "NMS Amiral Murgescu").

During the war, Romania copied and produced hundreds of Soviet [M1938 mortars](/wiki/M1938_mortar "M1938 mortar"),[[37]](#cite_note-auto-38) as well as designing and producing up to 400 [75 mm Reșița Model 1943](/wiki/75_mm_Re%C8%99i%C8%9Ba_Model_1943 "75 mm Reșița Model 1943") anti-tank guns. Infantry weapons designed and produced by Romania during the war include the [Orița M1941](/wiki/Ori%C8%9Ba_M1941 "Orița M1941") sub-machinegun and the [Argeș flamethrower](/wiki/Arge%C8%99_(flamethrower) "Argeș (flamethrower)"). Romania also built 30 [Vănătorul de care R-35](/wiki/Renault_R-35#Romania "Renault R-35"),[[40]](#cite_note-auto2-41) 34 [TACAM T-60](/wiki/TACAM_T-60 "TACAM T-60"), 21 [TACAM R-2](/wiki/TACAM_R-2 "TACAM R-2") tank destroyers and rebuilt 34 captured Soviet [Komsomolets armored tractors](/wiki/Komsomolets_armored_tractor "Komsomolets armored tractor").[[41]](#cite_note-42) A few prototype vehicles were also built, such as the [Mareșal tank destroyer](/wiki/Mare%C8%99al_tank_destroyer "Mareșal tank destroyer"), which is credited with being the inspiration for the German [Hetzer](/wiki/Hetzer "Hetzer"),[[40]](#cite_note-auto2-41) a [Renault R-35](/wiki/Renault_R-35 "Renault R-35") tank with a [T-26](/wiki/T-26 "T-26") turret[[40]](#cite_note-auto2-41) and an artillery tractor known as [T-1](/wiki/T-1_tractor "T-1 tractor"). Warships built include the submarines [NMS *Rechinul*](/wiki/NMS_Rechinul "NMS Rechinul") and [NMS *Marsuinul*](/wiki/NMS_Marsuinul "NMS Marsuinul"), [a class of 4 minesweepers](/wiki/Democra%C8%9Bia-class_minesweeper "Democrația-class minesweeper"), 6 Dutch-designed torpedo boats[[42]](#cite_note-43) and 2 gunboats.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Romania&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Romania_1930_literacy_EN.svg/250px-Romania_1930_literacy_EN.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Romania_1930_literacy_EN.svg)

[Literacy rate](/wiki/Literacy_rate "Literacy rate") in interwar Romania (1930)

While the Romanian nobility had a long tradition of sending their sons to Europe's finest schools, the educated were otherwise a tiny minority. Transylvania had the most educated population in Greater Romania, while Bessarabia fared the worst. While legally all Romanians were required to undergo at least four years of schooling, in practice few actually did and the system was designed to separate those who would go on to higher education from those who would not. While this was partially necessary due to limited resources, it ensured that peasants had almost no chance of becoming educated.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

High school and college education in Romania was modeled after the French system. Students undertook a rigid curriculum based around the liberal arts. Romania suffered from the same problem as the rest of Eastern Europe, which was that most students, coming from aristocratic backgrounds, preferred to study subjects such as theology, philosophy, literature and the fine arts over science, business, and engineering.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Administrative division

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Romania&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Administrative division")]

See also: [Historical administrative divisions of Romania](/wiki/Historical_administrative_divisions_of_Romania "Historical administrative divisions of Romania")

After Independence, the Romanian Old Kingdom was divided into 33 counties.

After World War I, as a result of the 1925 administrative unification law, the territory was divided into 71 counties, 489 districts (*[plăși](/wiki/Plas%C4%83 "Plasă")*) and 8,879 [communes](/wiki/Communes_of_Romania "Communes of Romania").

In 1938, [King Carol II](/wiki/Carol_II_of_Romania "Carol II of Romania") promulgated a new [Constitution](/wiki/1938_Constitution_of_Romania "1938 Constitution of Romania"), and subsequently he had the administrative division of the Romanian territory changed. Ten *ținuturi* (approximate translation: "lands") were created (by merging the counties) to be ruled by *rezidenți regali* (approximate translation: "Royal Residents") - appointed directly by the King. This administrative reform did not last and the counties were re-established after the fall of Carol's regime.

The [union of Bukovina with Romania](/wiki/Union_of_Bukovina_with_Romania "Union of Bukovina with Romania") is declared.  1 December. The [union of Transylvania with Romania](/wiki/Union_of_Transylvania_with_Romania "Union of Transylvania with Romania") is declared. This day concludes a series of unifications between the Kingdom of Romania and its claimed [historical regions](/wiki/Historical_regions_of_Romania "Historical regions of Romania"). However, the terms of these proclamations (and, subsequently, the materialization of the [Greater Romania](/wiki/Greater_Romania "Greater Romania") ideal) would only be de facto recognized 2 years later, following the [Treaty of Trianon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Trianon "Treaty of Trianon"). |
|  | By the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles"), Romania agreed to grant citizenship to the former citizens of Russian and Austro-Hungarian Empires living in the new Romanian territories.[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Kingdom_of_Romania#Dubious "Talk:Kingdom of Romania")*] |

[Little Entente](/wiki/Little_Entente "Little Entente") alliance with [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") and [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Yugoslavia "Kingdom of Yugoslavia") initiated. |
| • 1921 – | A major and radical [agrarian reform](/wiki/Agrarian_reform "Agrarian reform"). [Polish–Romanian alliance](/wiki/Polish%E2%80%93Romanian_alliance "Polish–Romanian alliance") established. |

| • 1930 – | Carol II crowned King. |
| • 1931 – | First ban on Iron Guard. |
| • 1933 – | 16 February. Grivița Railcar Workshops strike violently put down by police. 10 December. Prime Minister [Ion Duca](/wiki/Ion_Duca "Ion Duca") "dissolves" the Iron Guard, arresting thousands; 19 days later he is assassinated by Iron Guard legionnaires. |
| • 1935 – | LANC and National Agrarian Party merge to form the fascist National Christian Party (NCP). |

Romanian Communist Party denounces pact, but, in practice, supports the National-Peasants. LANC forms government, but is rapidly in conflict with Carol II over [his Jewish mistress](/wiki/Magda_Lupescu "Magda Lupescu"). |
| • 1938 – | 10 February. Royal dictatorship declared. New constitution adopted 27 February. 29–30 November. Iron Guard leader Codreanu and other legionnaires shot on the King's orders. |
| • 1939 – | 7 March. [Armand Călinescu](/wiki/Armand_C%C4%83linescu "Armand Călinescu") forms government. 23 August. [Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact](/wiki/Molotov%E2%80%93Ribbentrop_Pact "Molotov–Ribbentrop Pact") stipulates Soviet "interest" in Bessarabia. 1 September. Călinescu assassinated by Iron Guard legionnaires. |
| • 1940 – | 6 September. After the forced abdication of King Carol II, his 19-year-old son Michael I assumes the throne, being obliged to grant dictatorial powers to Prime Minister and [Conducător](/wiki/Conduc%C4%83tor "Conducător") [Ion Antonescu](/wiki/Ion_Antonescu "Ion Antonescu"). 14 September. The Kingdom of Romania is supplanted by a short-lived dictatorship called the [National Legionary State](/wiki/National_Legionary_State "National Legionary State"). |

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Kingdom_of_Romania&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-19)** The Treaty of Paris – recognizing the union with Bessarabia – never came into effect because one of its signatories, Japan, refused to ratify it. This meant that the union was not recognized by the international community, making it – unlike the other provinces – more of a *de facto* union than an official, *de jure* one.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Furthermore, President [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") left the peace conference to emphasize his disagreements earlier in 1919, and because the U.S. Congress did not ratify the [Treaty of Trianon](/wiki/Treaty_of_Trianon "Treaty of Trianon"), the United States of America and the Kingdom of Hungary signed a separate peace treaty on 29 August 1921.[[18]](#cite_note-18)
